title: What exactly is an LLM?
description: A Large Language Model (LLM) is fundamentally a statistical engine designed for next-token prediction, scaled up with massive amounts of data and compute.

sections.title: The Core Objective: Next-Token Prediction
sections.description: At its most basic level, an LLM takes a sequence of words (or 'tokens') as input and calculates the probability distribution for the next token. By repeatedly predicting the most likely next token and appending it to the input, the model generates coherent text. It is essentially an extremely sophisticated autocomplete.
sections.codeBlock:
# Conceptual representation of an LLM's core loop
def generate_text(prompt, max_tokens, model):
    current_text = prompt
    for _ in range(max_tokens):
        # Model predicts the probability of all possible next tokens
        next_token_probs = model.predict_next_token(current_text)
        
        # Sample the most likely token (e.g., greedy decoding)
        next_token = sample(next_token_probs)
        
        current_text += next_token
        if next_token == '<EOS>':
            break
            
    return current_text

sections.title: Learning Patterns, Not Just Statistics
sections.description: While early language models (like n-grams) relied on simple frequency counting, modern LLMs learn deep structural patterns. To accurately predict the next word in a complex sentence, the model is forced to learn grammar, syntax, reasoning, factual knowledge, and world context implicitly during its training phase.

sections.title: How it Scales: The Transformer
sections.description: The architecture that made LLMs possible is the Transformer. Unlike previous sequential models (like RNNs/LSTMs), Transformers process entire sequences in parallel using the Self-Attention mechanism. This allows the model to weigh the importance of every word in the context window relative to every other word, capturing long-range dependencies efficiently.

sections.title: The Training Pipeline
sections.description:
Creating a modern LLM involves three major stages:

1. Pre-training: The model is trained on a massive corpus of internet text to predict the next word. It learns world knowledge and language structure (creates a Base Model).
2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): The model is trained on high-quality instruction-response pairs to learn how to act as a helpful assistant rather than a document-completer.
3. Alignment (RLHF/DPO): The model is further fine-tuned to align with human preferences (e.g., being safe, harmless, and helpful) using reinforcement learning.

sections.title: End-to-End Architecture Diagram
1. Tokenizer & Input Embeddings
sections.description: The tokenizer converts raw text into a sequence of integer token IDs using a subword vocabulary (e.g., BPE or SentencePiece). Each token ID is mapped to a dense vector via an embedding lookup table of shape [vocab_size, d_model]. A positional encoding (sinusoidal or learned) is added to inject sequence position information, since self-attention has no inherent notion of order.
sections.codeBlock:
# Simplified embedding lookup + positional encoding
def embed_input(token_ids, embed_weight, max_len, d_model):
    B, T = token_ids.shape
    # Token embeddings: [B, T, d_model]
    tok_emb = embed_weight[token_ids]
    # Positional encoding: [1, max_len, d_model]
    pos = torch.arange(T).unsqueeze(0)
    pos_emb = sinusoidal_positional_encoding(pos, d_model)
    return tok_emb + pos_emb

sections.title: 2. Multi-Head Self-Attention
sections.description: Each token's hidden state is linearly projected into three matrices: Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V). The attention score between every pair of tokens is computed as Softmax(QK⁻⁄√d_k), producing a weighted sum of Values. This is split across multiple heads (typically 8–96) that learn different relationship types (syntax, semantics, positions). The outputs are concatenated and linearly projected back to d_model.
sections.codeBlock:
def multi_head_attention(x, num_heads, d_model):
    B, T, C = x.shape
    head_dim = C // num_heads
    
    # Project to Q, K, V: [B, T, C]
    Q = linear(x, W_Q)
    K = linear(x, W_K)
    V = linear(x, W_V)
    
    # Reshape for multi-head: [B, num_heads, T, head_dim]
    Q = Q.view(B, T, num_heads, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
    K = K.view(B, T, num_heads, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
    V = V.view(B, T, num_heads, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
    
    # Scaled dot-product attention
    scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
    attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)  # [B, h, T, T]
    
    # Weighted sum of Values
    out = attn @ V  # [B, h, T, d_h]
    
    # Concatenate heads and project
    out = out.transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
    return linear(out, W_O)

sections.title: 3. Feed-Forward Network (SwiGLU MLP)
sections.description:
Each token's hidden state passes independently through a two-layer MLP that expands the dimension by a factor of ×4 (or ×8/3 in SwiGLU). Modern LLMs use the SwiGLU activation: a gated variant where one path passes through SiLU (Swish) activation and is element-wise multiplied by another path. This accounts for roughly ⅔ of the model's parameters and compute.

Dimensions: d_model → intermediate_size (4d) → d_model
sections.codeBlock:
def swiglu_ffn(x, W_gate, W_up, W_down):
    # Gated linear unit with SiLU activation
    gate = F.silu(linear(x, W_gate))  # gating path
    up = linear(x, W_up)              # value path
    hidden = gate * up                 # element-wise gate
    return linear(hidden, W_down)      # project back to d_model

sections.title: 4. Residual Connections & Layer Normalization
sections.description: Every sub-layer (Attention and FFN) is wrapped in a residual connection followed by Layer Normalization. The residual path (output = sublayer(norm(x)) + x) allows gradients to flow directly through the network, preventing vanishing gradients in deep models. Pre-LayerNorm (applying norm before the sub-layer) is the standard in modern LLMs as it stabilizes training.
sections.codeBlock:
def transformer_block(x, attn, ffn, norm1, norm2):
    # Pre-LayerNorm architecture (modern standard)
    # Sub-layer 1: Multi-Head Self-Attention
    x = x + attn(norm1(x))
    
    # Sub-layer 2: Feed-Forward Network
    x = x + ffn(norm2(x))
    
    return x

sections.title: 5. LM Head & Output Sampling
sections.description: The final hidden state of the last transformer block passes through a LayerNorm and a linear projection (the LM Head) mapping from d_model to vocab_size logits. Softmax converts these logits into a probability distribution over the vocabulary. The next token is selected via argmax (greedy), top-k, top-p (nucleus), or temperature-scaled sampling strategies.
sections.codeBlock:
def lm_head(hidden_states, ln_final, head_weight):
    # Final layer norm
    h = ln_final(hidden_states[:, -1, :])  # only last token
    
    # Linear projection to vocabulary
    logits = h @ head_weight.T  # [1, vocab_size]
    
    # Temperature-scaled sampling
    probs = F.softmax(logits / temperature, dim=-1)
    
    # Sample from distribution (nucleus/top-k)
    next_token = sample_from_probs(probs, top_k=50, top_p=0.9)
    return next_token

sections.title: 6. KV Cache Integration
sections.description: During auto-regressive decoding, the KV Cache stores the Key and Value matrices from all previous attention steps. For each new token, only the new token's Q, K, V are computed, and the cached K, V are reused from memory instead of being recomputed across all previous positions. This reduces the per-step attention complexity from O(T²) to O(T), but at the cost of O(N × L × d) memory per sequence where N = num_heads, L = num_layers, d = head_dim.
sections.codeBlock:
def decode_with_kv_cache(token, past_kv, model):
    # past_kv holds cached K,V for all previous tokens
    
    # Compute Q, K, V only for the new token
    q = model.W_q(token)   # [1, 1, d_model]
    k = model.W_k(token)   # [1, 1, d_model]
    v = model.W_v(token)   # [1, 1, d_model]
    
    # Append to cache
    k = torch.cat([past_kv['k'], k], dim=1)
    v = torch.cat([past_kv['v'], v], dim=1)
    past_kv = {'k': k, 'v': v}
    
    # Attention over ALL cached positions (O(T) per step)
    scores = q @ k.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    out = attn @ v
    return out, past_kv

details:
Emergent Abilities: One of the most fascinating aspects of LLMs is that as you scale up the parameters (number of weights) and the training data, models begin to exhibit "emergent abilities"—skills they were never explicitly trained for, such as translating languages, writing code, or performing logical reasoning, simply because those patterns existed in the training data.

Key Design Principles: Modern LLMs follow the decoder-only transformer architecture popularized by GPT. The model is autoregressive — each token is generated one at a time, conditioned on all previous tokens. Scaling laws have shown consistent improvements with larger d_model, more layers, more data, and more compute. Key innovations include Pre-LayerNorm (GPT-2 onward), SwiGLU activations (PaLM, Llama), Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) (Llama, Mistral), and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) (Llama 2, Mistral) which reduces KV cache memory by sharing K/V heads across Q heads.
